question: Among the following complexes ( $\mathrm{K}$ to $\mathrm{P}$ ), $\mathrm{K}_{3}\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}\right](\mathrm{K}),\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{3}(\mathrm{~L})$, $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\left[\mathrm{Co}(\text { oxalate })_{3}\right](\mathrm{M}),\left[\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~N})$, $\mathrm{K}_{2}\left[\mathrm{Pt}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right](\mathrm{O})$, and $\left[\mathrm{Zn}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right]\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}(\mathrm{P})$, the diamagnetic complexes are:
explanation:
$[\mathrm{K}]: {{\text{K}}_{\text{3}}}\left[ {{\text{Fe(CN}}{{\text{)}}_{\text{6}}}} \right] : \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ has ${\text{3}}{{\text{d}}^{\text{5}}}$ configuration; $\mathrm{CN}^{-}$is strong field ligand and thus four electrons are paired leaving one unpaired electron with ${{\text{d}}^{\text{2}}}{\text{s}}{{\text{p}}^{\text{3}}}$ hybridisation and thus paramagnetic.
[L]: $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{3}: \mathrm{Co}^{3+}$ has configuration; $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is strong field ligand thus all six electrons are paired with ${{\text{d}}^{\text{2}}}{\text{s}}{{\text{p}}^{\text{3}}}$ hybridisation and therefore diamagnetic.
$[\mathrm{M}]: \mathrm{Na}_{3}\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{ox})_{3}\right]: \mathrm{Co}^{3+}$ configuration; $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-}$ is strong field ligand and thus all the six electrons are paired with ${{\text{d}}^{\text{2}}}{\text{s}}{{\text{p}}^{\text{3}}}$ hybridisation and therefore diamagnetic.
[N]: $\quad\left[\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{2}: \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}$ has ${\text{3}}{{\text{d}}^{\text{8}}}$ configuration with two unpaired electrons. $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ is weak field ligand and thus ${\text{s}}{{\text{p}}^{\text{3}}}{{\text{d}}^{\text{2}}}$ hybridisation and paramagnetic.
[O]: ${{\text{K}}_{\text{2}}}\left[ {{\text{Pt(CN}}{{\text{)}}_{\text{4}}}} \right]$. ${\text{;}}{{\text{P}}^{{\text{2 + }}}}$ has ${\text{3}}{{\text{d}}^{\text{8}}}$ Configuration. ${\text{C}}{{\text{N}}^{\text{ - }}}$ is strong ligand and thus all the eight electrons are paired with ${\text{ds}}{{\text{p}}^{\text{3}}}$ hybridiastion and therefore diamagnetic .
${\text{[P]:}}\left[ {{\text{Zn}}{{\left( {{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}} \right)}_{\text{6}}}{{\left( {{\text{N}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}} \right)}_{\text{2}}}{\text{:Z}}{{\text{n}}^{{\text{2 + }}}}} \right.$ has ${\text{3}}{{\text{d}}^{{\text{10}}}}$ Configuration and thus all paired showing ${\text{s}}{{\text{p}}^{\text{3}}}{{\text{d}}^{\text{2}}}$ hubridisation and so diamagnetic.

question: $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right]^{3+}$ is an inner orbital complex whereas $\left[\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right]^{2+}$ is an outer orbital complex because
explanation:
In the presence of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$, the $3 \mathrm{~d}$ electrons pair up leaving two d-orbitals empty to be involved in $\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{sp}^{3}$ hybridisation forming inner orbital complex in case of $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right]^{3+}$.
In $\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{6}{ }_{6}+$, $\mathrm{Ni}$ is in +2 oxidation state and has $\mathrm{d}^{8}$ configuration, the hybridisation involved is $\mathrm{sp}^{3} \mathrm{~d}^{2}$ forming outer orbital complex.

options.text: $\mathrm{Co}^{3+}$ has $\mathrm{d}^{6}$ whereas $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}$ has $\mathrm{d}^{8}$ hence pairing is not possible in $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}$
Ammonia behaves differently with both the metals
The approach of ligands to central metal ion is different.

question: The molecular formula of a coordinate complex is $\mathrm{CoH}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6} \mathrm{Cl}_{3}$. When one mole of this aqueous solution of complex is reacted with excess of aqueous $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ solution, three moles of AgCl was formed. What is the correct formula of the complex?
explanation:
$\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{3} \rightarrow\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right]^{3+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$
$3 \mathrm{Ag}^{+}+3 \mathrm{Cl}^{-} \rightarrow 3 \mathrm{AgCl}(\mathrm{s})$
Hence, the correct formula is $\left[\left(\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{3}\right.$.
subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
The compounds $\mathrm{CoCl}_{3} \cdot 6 \mathrm{NH}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{CoCl}_{3} .5 \mathrm{NH}_{3}$ can be distinguished from each other using conductivity measurements.
On adding excess $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$, $\mathrm{CoCl}_{3} .5 \mathrm{NH}_{3}$ gives 2 moles AgCl as precipitate.
explanation:
Statement A is true as conductivity measurements can differentiate the two compounds based on the number of ions they produce in solution. Statement B is true,
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{CoCl}_{3} \cdot 5 \mathrm{NH}_{3}+\underset{\text { (excess) }}{\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{AgCl}+ \\& {\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{Cl}\right]^{+2}}\end{aligned}$
Correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below:
Werner proposed that octahedral, tetrahedral, and square planar are common geometrical shapes in coordination compounds of transition metals.
Werner's theory suggests that the primary valences in coordination compounds are non-ionizable and are satisfied by neutral molecules.
explanation: Statement  A   is true as Werner indeed proposed common geometrical shapes for coordination compounds. Statement B is false because Werner's theory actually suggests that primary valences are ionizable and are usually satisfied by anions, not neutral molecules. Correct option is (2)

Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.

question: Out of the following complex compounds, which of the compound will be having the minimum conductance in solution?
explanation:
Conductance of any complex depends on the following factor:
(i) Number of ions produced by complex.
(ii) If number of ions are same then we will check charge on complex unit.
(1) $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{Cl}\right] \mathrm{Cl} \rightarrow\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{Cl}\right]^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$
(2) ${\left[ {{\rm{C}}{{\rm{o}}^{ + 3}}{{\left( {{\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_3}} \right)}_3}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{l}}_3}} \right]}$ - No ions (minimum conductance.)
(3) $\left[ {{\text{C}}{{\text{o}}^{ + 3}}{{\left( {{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_3}} \right)}_4}{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_2}} \right]{\text{Cl}} \to {\left[ {{\text{Co}}{{\left( {{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_3}} \right)}_4}{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_2}} \right]^ + } + {\text{C}}{{\text{l}}^ - }.$
(4) $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{3} \rightarrow\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right]^{+3}+3 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}$
subject: Chemistry

question: An octahedral complex with the formula ${\mathrm{\mathrm{CoCl}_{3} \cdot \mathrm{nNH}_{3}}}$ upon reaction with excess of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}}}$ solution gives 2 moles of AgCl .Consider the oxidation state of Co in the complex is x . The value of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{n}}}$ is ____.
explanation:
The given complex will have the structure ${\mathrm{\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{Cl}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{2}}}$ as it gives 2 moles of AgCl with ${\mathrm{\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}}}$. So, the value of ' ${\mathrm{n}}$ ' is 5.
${\mathrm{\therefore}}$ Oxidation state of cobalt is +3 , i.e., ${\mathrm{\mathrm{x}=3}}$
$\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{n}=8$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Chemistry

question: A coordination compound ${\mathrm{\mathrm{CrCl}_{3} .4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}}$ precipitates silver chloride with silver nitrate solution. The molar conductivity of the solution corresponds to a total number of two ions. The number of chloride ions within the coordination sphere of the compound is _____ .
explanation:
Since the coordination compound gives precipitate with silver nitrate, there should be at least one chloride ion outside the coordination sphere. Further, the conductivity of the compound corresponds to two ions. This implies, the formula of the compound will have:${\left[ {{\rm{complex}}\,{\rm{ion}}} \right]^ + } + {\rm{one}}\,{\rm{C}}{{\rm{l}}^ - }$ ion
So, the formula of the compound will be:
${\mathrm{\left[\mathrm{CrCl}_{2}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{4}\right] \mathrm{Cl}}}$
Therefore, the number of chloride ions within the coordination sphere of the compound is 2 .
subject: Chemistry

question: Palladium(II) tends to form complexes with a coordination number of 4 . One such compound was originally formulated as ${\mathrm{\mathrm{PdCl}_{2} \cdot 3 \mathrm{NH}_{3}}}$. Suppose an aqueous solution of the compound is treated with excess ${\mathrm{\mathrm{AgNO}_{3(\mathrm{aq})}}}$, how many moles of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{AgCl}_{(\mathrm{s})}}}$ are formed per mole of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{PdCl}_{2} \cdot 3 \mathrm{NH}_{3}}}$ ?
explanation:
Because of their charge, the ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Cl}^{-}}}$ions can be either in the coordination sphere, where they are bonded directly to the metal, or outside the coordination sphere, where they are bonded ionically to the complex. Because the ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}}$ ligands are neutral, they must be in the coordination sphere.
So, the correct formula is ${\mathrm{\left[\mathrm{Pd}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{Cl}\right] \mathrm{Cl}}}$.
The chloride ion that serves as a ligand will not be precipitated as ${\mathrm{\mathrm{AgCl}_{(\mathrm{s})}}}$. Thus, only the single ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Cl}^{\text {}}}}$, which is outside the coordination sphere can react with ${\mathrm{\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}}}$.
Therefore, 1 mol of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{AgCl}_{(\mathrm{s})}}}$ is formed per mole of the complex.
subject: Chemistry

So, the correct option is (3).

question: ${\rm{AgCl}}$ is dissolved in excess of each of ${\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{,KCN}}\,\,{\text{and}}{\kern 1pt} \,{\text{N}}{{\text{a}}_{\text{2}}}\,{{\text{S}}_{\text{2}}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}$. The complex ions produced in each case are
explanation:
On dissolving $\mathrm{AgCl}$ in excess of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}, \mathrm{KCN}$ and $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$, following complex ions are produced.
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{AgCl}+2 \mathrm{NH}_{3} \longrightarrow\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{+} \mathrm{Cl}^{-} \\& \mathrm{AgCl}+2 \mathrm{KCN} \longrightarrow \mathrm{K}^{+}\left[\mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{CN})_{2}\right]^{-}+\mathrm{KCl} \\& \mathrm{AgCl}+\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} \longrightarrow\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{3-}\end{aligned}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
Consider the following statements:
According to the Werner's theory.
(I) Ligands are connected to the metal ions by ionic bonds.
(II) Secondary valencies have directional properties
(III) Secondary valencies are non-ionizable. Of these statements:
explanation:
(I) Ligands are connected to the metal ion by coordinate covalent bond (dative bond).
(II) Secondary valences have directional properties.
(III) Secondary valences are non- ionizable and primary valences are ionizable.
So, the correct option is (2).

options.text: I, II and III are correct

two ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \rightarrow}}$ primary valency
one ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \rightarrow}}$ primary as well as secondary

options.text: All the Cl atoms show primary valency only.
Two Cl atoms show primary valency and one Cl atom shows secondary valency.
Two Cl atoms show primary valency and one Cl atom shows primary valency as well as secondary valency.

subject: Chemistry

question: Which of the following has the highest molar conductivity under similar conditions?
explanation:
${\mathrm{\left[\mathrm{Pt}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right] \rightarrow}}$ No.of ions in aq. solution is 0.
${\mathrm{\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{4} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right] \rightarrow}}$ No. of ions in aq. solution is 2 .
${\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{4}\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}\right] \rightarrow}}$ No. of ions in aq. solution is 5
${\mathrm{\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{3} \rightarrow}}$ No. of ions in aq. solution is 4 .
So, ${\mathrm{\mathrm{K}_{4}\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}\right]}}$ will show highest conductivity, as it possesses highest no. of ions.
subject: Chemistry

question:
Select CORRECT statements about $\mathrm{PtCl}_{4}$. $4 \mathrm{NH}_{3}$ complex salt
I. All the chlorides are bonded by primary valency.
II. All the chlorides are bonded by secondary valency.
III. All the $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ are bonded by secondary valency.
IV. Half of the chlorides are bonded with secondary valency.
explanation: ${\mathrm{\left[\mathrm{Pt}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{4} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{2}}}$ So, all four ammonia molecules and half of chloride ions (2) satisfy secondary valency.
subject: Chemistry

question: On treating 100 mL of 0.1 M aqueous solution of the complex ${\mathrm{\mathrm{CrCl}_{3} \cdot 6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}}$ with excess of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}, 2.86 \mathrm{~g}}}$ of AgCl was obtained. The complex is
explanation:
No. of moles of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{AgCl}=\dfrac{2.86}{143}=0.02}}$ moles
0.01 moles of an aqueous solution of${\mathrm{\mathrm{CrCl}_{3} \cdot 6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}}$ gives 0.02 moles AgCl means, 1 mole of aqueous solution gives 2 moles of AgCl .
so, ${\mathrm{2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}}}$ions should satisfy only primary valency and the compound will be ${\mathrm{\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{5} \mathrm{Cl}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{2} \cdot \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
A $1 \mathrm{~L}, 0.02 \mathrm{M}$ solution of $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{SO}_{4}\right] \mathrm{Br}$ is mixed with $1 \mathrm{~L}, 0.02 \mathrm{M}$ solution of $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{Br}\right] \mathrm{SO}_{4}$. The resulting solution is divided into two equal parts $(\mathrm{X})$ and treated with excess of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ solution and $\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$ solution, respectively as shown below:
1L Solution ( X$)+\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ solution (excess)
$ \to {\rm{Y}}$
1L Solution ( X$)+\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$ solution (excess)
$\longrightarrow$ Z
The number of moles of Y and Z , respectively are
explanation:
1.0 L of mixture X contain 0.01 mole of each $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{SO}_{4}\right] \mathrm{Br}$ and $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{Br}\right] \mathrm{SO}_{4}$. With $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$, only $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{SO}_{4}\right] \mathrm{Br}$ reacts giving AgBr precipitate as :
$\left[\underset{\substack{0.1 \text { mol }}}{\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{SO}_{4}}\right] \mathrm{Br}+\underset{\text { (Excess) }}{\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}} \rightarrow$ $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{SO}_{4}\right] \mathrm{NO}_{3}+\underset{0.01 \mathrm{~mol}}{\mathrm{AgBr}}$
Similarly, with $\mathrm{BaCl}_{2},\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{Br}\right] \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ reacts giving $\mathrm{BaSO}_{4}$ precipitate.
$\mathop {\left[ {{\rm{Co}}{{\left( {{\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_3}} \right)}_5}{\rm{Br}}} \right]{\rm{S}}{{\rm{O}}_4}}\limits_{0.01\,{\rm{mol}}} + \mathop {{\rm{BaC}}{{\rm{l}}_2}}\limits_{{\rm{ (Excess) }}} \to $
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left[ {{\rm{Co}}{{\left( {{\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_3}} \right)}_5}{\rm{Br}}} \right]{\rm{C}}{{\rm{l}}_2} + \mathop {{\rm{BaS}}{{\rm{O}}_4}}\limits_{0.01\,{\rm{mol}}} $
Hence, the correct answer is option (3).
subject: Chemistry

question: The primary and secondary valencies of cobalt, respectively in $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{Cl}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ are
explanation:
Primary valency is the charge on central metal ion.
Let the charge on Co in the complex,
$\left[ {{\text{Co}}{{\left( {{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_3}} \right)}_5}{\text{Cl}}} \right]{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_2}\,\,{\text{be}}\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\text{x}}$
${\rm{x}} + 5\,(0) - 3 = 0$
${\rm{x}} = + \,3$
Secondary valency is the number of ligands in the complex i.e., 6 .
subject: Chemistry

question: The co-ordination number of metal $\mathrm{M}$ in a complex having formula $\mathrm{MCl}_{3} .4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ is 6 and there is no molecule of hydration in it. The volume of $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ solution needed to precipitate the free chloride ions in $200 \mathrm{ml}$ of $0.01 \mathrm{M}$ complex is:
explanation:
Molecule has the formula
$=\left[\mathrm{M}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{4} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right] \mathrm{Cl}$
$\left[\mathrm{M}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{4} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right] \mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$
$\rightarrow \mathrm{AgCl}+\left[\mathrm{M}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{4} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right] \mathrm{NO}_{3}$
Complex given $=200 \mathrm{ml}$ of $0.01 \mathrm{M}=2$ millimole
$=2 \times 10^{-3}$ moles
$\therefore \mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ required $=2 \times 10^{-3}$ moles
Molarity of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}=0.1 \mathrm{M}$ (given)
So, volume required is
$\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{ml})=$ no.of moles $\times \dfrac{1000}{\mathrm{M}}$
$=\dfrac{2 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000}{0.1}=20 \mathrm{ml}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Which of the following statement is incorrect?
explanation: -ve ligands can satisfy primary valencies of metal but neutral ligands cannot satisfy primary valency. In option (1), $\mathrm{CN}^{-}$ligands satisfy primary valencies also.

options.text: In $\mathrm{Fe}_{4}\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}\right]_{3}$, the ligands have satisfied only the secondary valencies of ferrous ion.
In $\mathrm{K}_{3}\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}\right]$, the ligands have satisfied both primary and secondary valencies of ferric ion.
In $\mathrm{K}_{4}\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}\right]$, the ligands have satisfied both primary and secondary valencies of ferrous ion.
In $\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{4}\right] \mathrm{SO}_{4}$, the ligands have satisfied only the secondary valencies of copperion.

subject: Chemistry

question: No of ionizable & non-ionizable $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ions in $\mathrm{CoCl}_{3} \cdot 5 \mathrm{NH}_{3}$ respectively are:
explanation: The compound will be $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \cdot \mathrm{Cl}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$. So ionizable valency is 2 and non-ionizable valency is 1 .
subject: Chemistry

question: Which of the following does not give a precipitate either with $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ or with $\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$ ?
explanation: $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{Cl}_{3}\right]$ is a neutral complex. So, it can't give any test with $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ or with $\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: Which of the following is not a drawback of Werner's theory?
explanation: Werner's theory can explain the valency of metal ions but it can't explain colour and magnetic behaviour in complex compounds.

options.text: Does not explain the valency of metal ions in the complex.
Does not give any explanation for the colour of complex compounds.
Does not explain the magnetic behaviour of complex compounds.
Does not correlate electronic configuration of the metal with the formation of complex.

subject: Chemistry

question:
Match the column I with column II and choose the correct option.
A $\mathrm{CoCl}_{3} \cdot 6 \mathrm{NH}_{3}$
B $\mathrm{CoCl}_{3} \cdot 5 \mathrm{NH}_{3}$
C $\mathrm{CoCl}_{3} \cdot 4 \mathrm{NH}_{3}$
D $\mathrm{CoCl}_{3} \cdot 3 \mathrm{NH}_{3}$
P Violet
Q Green

S Yellow

${\text{A - Q,   B - P,   C - S,   D - R}}$
${\text{A - R,   B - P,   C - S,   D - Q}}$
${\text{A - S,   B - R,   C - Q,   D - P}}$

subject: Chemistry

question: Addition of excess of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ to an aqueous solution of 1 mole of $\mathrm{PdCl}_{2} \cdot 4 \mathrm{NH}_{3}$ gives 2 moles of $\mathrm{AgCl}$. The conductivity of this solution corresponds to
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned}& {\left[\mathrm{Pd}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{4}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{2}+\mathrm{AgNO}_{3} \rightarrow\left[\mathrm{Pd}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{4}\right]^{+2}} \\& +2 \mathrm{AgCl}\end{aligned}$$
So 1:2 ratio of electrolyte responsible for conductance.
subject: Chemistry

question: The secondary valency of platinum in tetrammine dichloroplatinum(IV) chloride is
explanation: Secondary valency corresponds to the coordination number of the central metal atom or ion. Coordination number of $\mathrm{Pt}$ in $\left[\mathrm{Pt}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{4} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right] \mathrm{Cl}$ is 6 .
subject: Chemistry

question: According to Werner's theory, the geometry of the complex is determined by
explanation: Werner's theory was used to describe the structure and formation of complex compounds or coordination compounds. According to this theory the primary valency gives the oxidation number and the secondary valency gives the coordination number. Also, the geometry of the complex is determined by number and position of secondary valences in space as the ligand satisfying seconday valences are always directed towards fixed position in space.

options.text: only from the primary valence in space
number and position of the primary valency  in space
number and position of the secondary valency  in space
only from the position of secondary valence in space

subject: Chemistry

question: Which complex among the following gives a white precipitate on treatment with an aqueous solution of barium chloride?
explanation:
Among the given coordination compounds, $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{NO}_{2}\right] \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ gives a white precipitate on treatment with an aqueous solution of barium chloride. This can be explained by the following reaction.
$$\begin{aligned}& {\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{NO}_{2}\right] \mathrm{SO}_{4}+\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}(\mathrm{aq})} \\& \quad \longrightarrow\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{NO}_{2}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{2}+\underset{\text { Whiteppt. }}{\mathrm{BaSO}_{4}(\mathrm{~s})}\end{aligned}$$
The other given compounds do not give white ppt. due to absence of $\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}$ ions.
subject: Chemistry

question: What is the secondary valence of $\mathrm{Co}^{3+}$ ion according to Werner's theory in  $$\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{4} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]^{+} \text {? }$$
explanation: In the complex $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{4} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]^{+}$, the secondary valence of $\mathrm{Co}^{3+}$ ion according to Werner's theory is 6 . The secondary valences are non-ionisable. These are satisfied by neutral molecules or negative ions. The secondary valence is equal to the coordination number and is fixed for a metal.
subject: Chemistry

question: Which complex cannot ionise in solution?
explanation: $\left[\mathrm{CoCl}_{3}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{3}\right]$ cannot ionise in solution as it is a non-electrolyte and do not furnish ions in solution.
subject: Chemistry

question:
The correct order of the stoichiometries of $\mathrm{AgCl}$ formed when $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ in excess is treated with the complexs:
$\mathrm{CoCl}_{3} .6 \mathrm{NH}_{3}, \mathrm{CoCl}_{3} .5 \mathrm{NH}_{3}, \mathrm{CoCl}_{3} .4 \mathrm{NH}_{3}$ respectively is :-
explanation:
$\left[ {{\text{Co}}{{\left( {{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}} \right)}_{\text{6}}}} \right]{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{3}}}\xrightarrow{{{\text{AgN}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}}}{\text{3}}\,{\text{mol AgCl}}$
$\left[ {{\text{Co}}{{\left( {{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}} \right)}_{\text{5}}}{\text{Cl}}} \right]{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}\xrightarrow{{{\text{AgN}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}}}{\text{2}}\,{\text{mol AgCl}}$
$\left[ {{\text{Co}}{{\left( {{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}} \right)}_{\text{4}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}} \right]{\text{Cl}}\xrightarrow{{{\text{AgN}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}}}{\text{1}}\,{\text{mol AgCl}}$
subject: Chemistry

question:
Which of the following complex can be ionised in solution?
I. $\left[\mathrm{CoCl}_{3}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{3}\right]$
II. $\left[\mathrm{Pt}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{4}$
III. $\mathrm{K}_{2}\left[\mathrm{PtF}_{6}\right]$
IV. $\mathrm{K}_{4}\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}\right]$
explanation: Only $\left[\mathrm{CoCl}_{3}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{3}\right]$ complex does not ionised in solution, as no ion is present outside the coordination sphere.
subject: Chemistry

question: Which one of the following complexes will consume more equivalents of aqueous solution of $\operatorname{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)$ ?
explanation: Complex $\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{3}$ will consume more equivalents of aqueous solution of $\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)$.
subject: Chemistry

question: Both $\mathrm{Co}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{Pt}^{4+}$ have a coordination number of six. Which of the following pair of complexes will show approximately the same electrical conductance for their $0.001 \mathrm{M}$ aqueous solution? (Assume 100 % ionisation)
explanation:
$\mathrm{CoCl}_{3} \cdot 6 \mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is represented as $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{3}$
Number of ions $=4$
$\mathrm{PtCl}_{4} .5 \mathrm{NH}_{3}$ is represented as $\left[\mathrm{Pt}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{Cl}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{3}$ Number of ions $=4$
subject: Chemistry

question: On treatment of $100 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ solution of $\mathrm{CoCl}_{3} \cdot 6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ with exces $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3} ; 1.2 \times 10^{22}$ ions are precipitated . The complex is :-
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned}& \text { Moles of complex }=\dfrac{\text { Molarity } \times \text { volume }(\mathrm{ml})}{1000} \\& =\dfrac{100 \times 0.1}{1000}=0.01 \mathrm{~mole}\end{aligned}$$
Moles of ions precipitated with excess of
$$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{AgNO}_{3}=\dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{22}}{6.02 \times 10^{23}} \\& =0.02 \text { moles }\end{aligned}$$
Number of $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$present in ionization sphere
${\text{ = }}\frac{{{\text{Mole of ion precipitated with excess AgN}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}}}{{{\text{mole}}\,\,{\text{of}}\,\,{\text{complex}}}}$
$$=\dfrac{0.02}{0.01}=2$$
It means $2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ions present in ionization sphere
$$\therefore \text { complex is }\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{5} \mathrm{Cl}_{3}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{2} \cdot \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$$
subject: Chemistry

question: An excess of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ is added to $100 \mathrm{~mL}$ of a 0.01 M solution of dichlorotetraaquachromium (III) chloride. The number of moles of $\mathrm{AgCl}$ precipitated would be
explanation:
$\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}+\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{4} \mathrm{Cl}\right] \mathrm{Cl} \rightarrow$
$$\mathrm{AgCl}+\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right] \mathrm{NO}_{3}\right.$$
millimole of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ added is
$$100 \times 0.1=1$$
$\therefore$ Mole of $\mathrm{AgCl}=1 \times 10^{-3}=0.001$
(As $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ causes ppt of $\mathrm{AgCl}$ so $\mathrm{AgCl}$ ppt is $10^{-3}$ moles)
subject: Chemistry

question:
Assume $100 \%$ ionisation of the following in aq. solution
(I) $\left[\mathrm{Pt}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{4}$
(II) $\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{3}$
(III) $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{4}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$
(IV) $\mathrm{NaCl}$
Increasing order of conductivity of the above solutions is
explanation: More number of ions more is the conductivity.
subject: Chemistry

question: According to Werner's theory, the primary valencies of the central metal atom
explanation: According to Werner's theroy, the primary valencies of the central metal atom are satisfied by negative ions.

options.text: are satisfied by negative ions or neutral molecules
are equal to its coordination number
decide the  geometry of the complex.

subject: Chemistry

question: A six coordinate complex of formula $\mathrm{CrCl}_{3} 6 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ has green colour. A $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ solution of the complex when treated with excess of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ gave $28.7 \mathrm{~g}$ of white precipitate. The formula of the complex would be:
explanation:
$\begin{gathered} {\text{CrC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{3}}} \times {\text{6}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O + AgN}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}} \to {\text{AgCl }} \hfill \\ \,\,\,{\text{0}}.{\text{1}}\,{\text{mol}}\quad {\text{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\text{excess }}\quad {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\text{28}}.{\text{7}}\,{\text{g}} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} $
$$\dfrac{28.7}{143.5}=0.2 \mathrm{~mol}$$
Hence, the formula of the complex would be
$$\left[\mathrm{CrCl}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{5}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$$
subject: Chemistry

question: When $0.1\,{\rm{mol }}\,\,{\rm{CoC}}{{\rm{l}}_3}{\left( {{\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_3}} \right)_5}$  is treated with excess of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}, 0.2 \mathrm{~mol}$ of $\mathrm{AgCl}$ is obtained. The conductivity of solution will correspond to
subject: Chemistry

question: A coordination compound of cobalt has molecular formula containing five ammonia molecules, one nitro group and two chlorine atoms for one cobalt atom. One mole of this compound produces three mole ions in an aqueous solution. On reacting this solution, with excess of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ solution two moles of $\mathrm{AgCl}$ get precipitated. The ionic formula of this complex would be
explanation:
Since the complex with $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ solution gives two moles of $\mathrm{AgCl}$ it must have two ionisable $\mathrm{C} 1$ atoms. Hence the complex is $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{NO}_{2}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$. On ionisation it will give three moles of ion viz
$\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]^{2+}$ and $2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}$
subject: Chemistry

question: An excess of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ is added to $100 \mathrm{~mL}$ of a $0.01 \mathrm{M}$ solution of $\left[\mathrm{CrCl}_{2}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{4}\right] \mathrm{Cl}$. The number of moles of $\mathrm{AgCl}$ precipitated would be:
explanation:
$\left[\mathrm{CrCl}_{2}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{4}\right] \mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{AgNO}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{AgCl}$
Number of moles of complex $=0.1 \times 0.01$
$=0.001 \mathrm{~mole}$
1 mole of complex gives 1 mole of $\mathrm{AgCl}$
0.001 mole of complex gives 0.001 mole of $\mathrm{AgCl}$
subject: Chemistry

question: Which of the following will have greatest molar conductance at infinite dilution?
explanation: More number of ions, more conductance
subject: Chemistry

question: A coordination compound $\mathrm{CrCl}_{3} \cdot 4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ precipitates silver chloride when treated with silver nitrate. The molar conductance of its solution corresponds to a total of two ions. The structural formula of the compound is
explanation: Only option (1) has two ions
subject: Chemistry

question: Aqueous solution of $\mathrm{CoCl}_{3} \cdot 6 \mathrm{NH}_{3}$ upon addition with $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ produces 3 moles white precipitate. Primary and secondary valency of metal in this complex is
explanation: Formula of the complex based on the given data is $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{6}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{3}$.
subject: Chemistry

question: Pick a poor electrolytic conductor complex in solution
explanation: The complex furnishing least number of ions in solution will be poor electrolytic conductor.
subject: Chemistry

question:
On the basis of the following observations made with aqueous solutions, assign secondary valencies to metals in the following compounds:
$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  \begin{gathered}
  \,\,\,\,{\mathbf{Formula}} \hfill \\
  {\mathbf{precipitated}} \hfill \\ 
\end{gathered} &\begin{gathered}
  {\mathbf{Moles}}\,\,{\mathbf{of}}\,\,{\mathbf{AgCl}} \hfill \\
  {\mathbf{per}}\,\,{\mathbf{mole}}\,\,{\mathbf{of}}\,\,{\mathbf{the}} \hfill \\
  {\mathbf{compounds}}\,\,{\mathbf{with}} \hfill \\
  {\mathbf{excess}}\,\,{\mathbf{AgN}}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{3}}} \hfill \\ 
\end{gathered}  \\ 
  {{\text{(i)}}\,\,{\text{PdC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{.4N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}}&{\text{2}} \\ 
  {{\text{(ii)}}\,\,{\text{NiC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{.6}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}}&2 \\ 
  {{\text{(iii)}}\,\,{\text{PtC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{4}}}{\text{.2N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}}&0 \\ 
  {{\text{(iv)}}\,\,{\text{CoC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{.4N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}}&1 \\ 
  {{\text{(v)}}\,\,{\text{PtC}}{{\text{l}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{.2N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}}&0 
\end{array}$
explanation:
Number of moles of $\mathrm{AgCl}$ precipitated denotes number of $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ions, and the primary valency. Therefore,
${\rm{(i)PdC}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{.4N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}$ is $\left[ {{\rm{Pd}}{{\left( {{\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}} \right)}_{\rm{4}}}} \right]{\rm{C}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{2}}}$, secondary valency is 4.
${\rm{(ii)NiC}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{.6}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}$ is $\left[ {{\rm{Ni}}{{\left( {{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}} \right)}_{\rm{6}}}} \right]{\rm{C}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{2}}}$, secondary valency is 6.
${\rm{(iii)}}\,{\rm{PtC}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{.2N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}$ is $\left[ {{\rm{Pt}}{{\left( {{\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}} \right)}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{4}}}} \right]$ with secondary valency of zero.
${\rm{(iv)}}\,{\rm{CoC}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{.4N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}$ is $\left[ {{\rm{Co}}{{\left( {{\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}} \right)}_{\rm{4}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{2}}}} \right]{\rm{Cl}}$ having secondary valency of 6.
${\rm{(v)}}\,{\rm{PtC}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{.2N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}$ is $\left[ {{\rm{Pt}}{{\left( {{\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}} \right)}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{C}}{{\rm{l}}_{\rm{2}}}} \right]$, secondary valency of zero.
subject: Chemistry

question: When $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ is added to a solution of $\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{Cl}_{3}$, the precipitate of $\mathrm{AgCl}$ shows two ionisable chloride ions. This means:
explanation:
Since the precipitate of $\mathrm{AgCl}$ shows two ionisable chloride ion the complex must have the structure.
$$\begin{aligned}& {\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{Cl}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{2}+2 \mathrm{AgNO}_{3} } \longrightarrow \\& {\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{Cl}\right]\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}+2 \mathrm{AgCl} }\end{aligned}$$
Hence two chlorine atoms satisfy the primary valency and one, secondary valency.

options.text: Two chlorine atoms satisfy primary valency and one secondary valency
One chlorine atom satisfies primary as well as secondary valency
Three chlorine atoms satisfy primary valency
Three chlorine atoms satisfy secondary valency

subject: Chemistry
